When legislators and judges impose policies that directly contradict the clear, repeatedly expressed will of the people, they engage in an elite form of paternalism that undermines democratic consent. Abolition is not an organic Nigerian position arising from local debate. It is an imported ideology pushed by foreign governments, European-funded NGOs, and a small class of Western-educated elites who prioritize alignment with global human-rights fashions over the security realities and values of their own citizens. These advocates essentially ask Nigerian institutions to override what Nigerians themselves overwhelmingly want in favor of preferences shaped in Brussels, Geneva, and London—places that do not face Fulani militancy, banditry on the Abuja-Kaduna highway, or schoolchildren abducted en masse.

Respecting democratic legitimacy does not mean following every poll blindly, but when surveys show such decisive and stable majorities on a life-and-death issue that directly affects public safety, the burden properly shifts to abolitionists to justify why they should be allowed to nullify the popular will. They have not met that burden. Instead, they cloak anti-democratic imposition in the language of “progress” and “civilization,” implying that the Nigerian people are too backward to understand human rights. This condescension should be rejected outright. Nigeria is a sovereign democracy. Its criminal-justice policy must reflect the informed judgment of its own population, not the ideological priorities of external donors. ### Constitutional and Legal Foundations
Abolitionists routinely speak as though the death penalty is an unconstitutional relic or a violation of fundamental rights. This claim is flatly false and ignores the explicit text of Nigeria’s supreme law. Section 33(1) of the 1999 Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria states without ambiguity: “Every person has a right to life, and no one shall be deprived intentionally of his life, save in execution of the sentence of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty in Nigeria.” The right to life is expressly qualified by the state’s authority to impose capital punishment after due process. The framers of the Constitution deliberately preserved this power; they did not leave it open to judicial invention or foreign pressure.

The Supreme Court of Nigeria has repeatedly affirmed this plain meaning. In the landmark case of *Kalu v. State* (1998) 13 NWLR (Pt. 583) 1, the apex court upheld the constitutionality of the death penalty, rejecting arguments that it amounted to cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment under Section 34. The Court made clear that capital punishment is compatible with Nigeria’s constitutional order and cannot be wished away by creative reinterpretation. Lower courts and subsequent decisions have followed this precedent. Any serious attempt to abolish the death penalty would therefore require a constitutional amendment under the rigorous provisions of Section 9—an arduous process demanding two-thirds majorities in both houses of the National Assembly and approval by at least 24 state houses of assembly. Abolitionists are not merely offering policy advice; they are demanding that Nigeria rewrite its foundational document to suit an ideology that failed to win popular or legislative support.

Nigeria’s plural legal system further entrenches capital punishment. The 12 northern states that adopted Sharia penal codes in the early 2000s explicitly retained and expanded capital offenses under Islamic criminal law, including for hudud crimes such as murder and certain forms of armed robbery. These codes reflect the deeply held values of tens of millions of Nigerian Muslims who form a substantial part of the federation. To abolish the death penalty nationwide would not only override the democratic will and the explicit constitutional text; it would also constitute a direct assault on the religious and legal autonomy guaranteed to those states under the federal bargain. Such an imposition would inflame religious tensions and undermine the very pluralism that defines the Nigerian state.

The death penalty is therefore not an aberration or a “colonial hangover.” It is explicitly embedded in Nigeria’s supreme law, repeatedly validated by the Supreme Court, and integrated into both common-law and Sharia traditions. Abolitionists who pressure Nigeria to suspend or repeal it are asking the country to abandon its own Constitution, disregard its highest court, and override the religious laws chosen by a large segment of its population. That is not progressive reform; it is constitutional subversion dressed in the language of human rights.

- Public execution by firing squad or hanging for maximum deterrent visibility, conducted with dignity and recorded to prevent abuse.
- Transparent reporting of cases, evidence, and appeals to build public confidence.
- Parallel investment in police forensics, judicial capacity, and prison reform so that the system can reliably separate the guilty from the innocent.

The Nigerian people have suffered enough. The blood of schoolchildren slaughtered in their classrooms, farmers butchered in their fields, and travelers executed on the roadside demands a response proportionate to the evil inflicted. Sentimentality dressed up as sophistication has no place here. The state exists to protect the innocent and punish the guilty. When the guilty have committed crimes of ultimate wickedness, the only proportionate punishment is death.

Nigeria should not merely retain the death penalty on its statutes. It should apply it actively, justly, and without apology. The survival of the nation, the security of its people, fidelity to democratic legitimacy, and obedience to its own Constitution require nothing less.

Abolition is not an organic Nigerian position arising from local debate. It is an imported ideology pushed by foreign governments, European-funded NGOs, and a small class of Western-educated elites who prioritize alignment with global human-rights fashions over the security realities and values of their own citizens. These advocates essentially ask Nigerian institutions to override what Nigerians themselves overwhelmingly want in favor of preferences shaped in Brussels, Geneva, and London—places that do not face Fulani militancy, banditry on the Abuja-Kaduna highway, or schoolchildren abducted en masse.

Respecting democratic legitimacy does not mean following every poll blindly, but when surveys show such decisive and stable majorities on a life-and-death issue that directly affects public safety, the burden properly shifts to abolitionists to justify why they should be allowed to nullify the popular will. They have not met that burden. Instead, they cloak anti-democratic imposition in the language of “progress” and “civilization,” implying that the Nigerian people are too backward to understand human rights. This condescension should be rejected outright. Nigeria is a sovereign democracy. Its criminal-justice policy must reflect the informed judgment of its own population, not the ideological priorities of external donors. Active enforcement of the death penalty is not only just and effective; it is the policy that enjoys authentic democratic legitimacy.

### Constitutional and Legal Foundations
Abolitionists routinely speak as though the death penalty is an unconstitutional relic or a violation of fundamental rights. This claim is flatly false and ignores the explicit text of Nigeria’s supreme law. Section 33(1) of the 1999 Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria states without ambiguity: “Every person has a right to life, and no one shall be deprived intentionally of his life, save in execution of the sentence of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty in Nigeria.” The right to life is expressly qualified by the state’s authority to impose capital punishment after due process. The framers of the Constitution deliberately preserved this power; they did not leave it open to judicial invention or foreign pressure.

The Supreme Court of Nigeria has repeatedly affirmed this plain meaning. In the landmark case of *Kalu v. State* (1998) 13 NWLR (Pt. 583) 1, the apex court upheld the constitutionality of the death penalty, rejecting arguments that it amounted to cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment under Section 34. The Court made clear that capital punishment is compatible with Nigeria’s constitutional order and cannot be wished away by creative reinterpretation. Lower courts and subsequent decisions have followed this precedent. Any serious attempt to abolish the death penalty would therefore require a constitutional amendment under the rigorous provisions of Section 9—an arduous process demanding two-thirds majorities in both houses of the National Assembly and approval by at least 24 state houses of assembly. Abolitionists are not merely offering policy advice; they are demanding that Nigeria rewrite its foundational document to suit an ideology that failed to win popular or legislative support.

Nigeria’s plural legal system further entrenches capital punishment. The 12 northern states that adopted Sharia penal codes in the early 2000s explicitly retained and expanded capital offenses under Islamic criminal law, including for hudud crimes such as murder and certain forms of armed robbery. These codes reflect the deeply held values of tens of millions of Nigerian Muslims who form a substantial part of the federation. To abolish the death penalty nationwide would not only override the democratic will and the explicit constitutional text; it would also constitute a direct assault on the religious and legal autonomy guaranteed to those states under the federal bargain. Such an imposition would inflame religious tensions and undermine the very pluralism that defines the Nigerian state.

The death penalty is therefore not an aberration or a “colonial hangover.” It is explicitly embedded in Nigeria’s supreme law, repeatedly validated by the Supreme Court, and integrated into both common-law and Sharia traditions. Abolitionists who pressure Nigeria to suspend or repeal it are asking the country to abandon its own Constitution, disregard its highest court, and override the religious laws chosen by a large segment of its population. That is not progressive reform; it is constitutional subversion dressed in the language of human rights.

### Victim Justice and Closure for Survivors
No argument for capital punishment is more compelling than the raw, unending pain of victims and their families. Nigeria carries a staggering backlog of atrocities that cry out for final justice: the 276 Chibok schoolgirls abducted in 2014, many of whom remain missing or enslaved; the Dapchi girls kidnapped in 2018; the Owo church massacre of June 2022, where terrorists slaughtered more than 40 worshippers during Pentecost Sunday Mass; the Plateau Christmas massacres of 2023 that claimed around 200 lives in coordinated attacks on defenseless farming communities; and the countless bandit raids across the Northwest in which entire families have been wiped out—parents shot, children burned alive, survivors left to bury their dead while perpetrators roam free or negotiate comfortable “amnesty” deals.

These survivors and bereaved families do not need empty lectures about “restorative justice,” “rehabilitation,” or the supposed “dignity” of mass murderers. They deserve the finality that only execution can deliver. Watching a terrorist commander or bandit leader live out his days in prison—eating state-provided food, receiving medical care, and possibly continuing to issue orders—re-traumatizes victims every single day. It tells them their loved ones’ lives were cheap, that the Nigerian state values the comfort of killers above the grief of the innocent. Restorative frameworks are grotesquely inappropriate when applied to ideologically driven terrorists or professional mass killers who have shown no capacity for remorse and whose crimes were calculated to shatter entire communities. There is no “restoring” the parents of a beheaded child or the congregation that watched its priest slaughtered at the altar. Closure demands proportionate justice: the lawful, deliberate ending of the perpetrator’s life.

Anything less is moral cowardice disguised as compassion. Abolitionists who insist on preserving the lives of these monsters while Nigeria’s widows and orphans receive no meaningful reparations are not defending human rights—they are elevating the rights of predators over the rights of the prey. Victim justice must be the North Star of any serious criminal-justice policy. In Nigeria’s context of mass atrocity, that star points unmistakably toward the active, unapologetic use of the death penalty.

### Responding to Common Objections
Abolitionists call the death penalty “barbaric.” The true barbarism is forcing the mothers of Chibok girls or the survivors of the Owo massacre to share the same planet with the unpunished architects of their suffering while abolitionist elites sip imported wine and lecture Africans about civilization. Nigerian victims know barbarism firsthand—burning villages, beheaded farmers, raped schoolgirls. The state that recoils from executing the guilty but tolerates their continued existence at public expense practices a selective mercy that is itself obscene.

Critics invoke the risk of executing the innocent. This concern deserves rigorous safeguards—better forensics, mandatory double appeals, state-funded counsel, and the highest evidentiary bar—but it cannot justify blanket abolition. The guilt of Boko Haram commanders filmed executing hostages, or bandit leaders identified by multiple surviving witnesses in Plateau massacres, is not in serious doubt. To paralyze justice for the clearly guilty out of hypothetical fear for the wrongly accused is to make perfect the enemy of good while real victims continue to suffer. Nigeria must reform its institutions, not surrender its constitutional right to impose final punishment.

The claim that “poverty causes crime, so punish root causes instead” is a contemptible evasion. Every society has poor people; few tolerate the industrial-scale slaughter Nigeria now endures. Socio-economic programs are vital, but they do not disarm terrorists today. Security is the prerequisite for development. Pretending otherwise while victims wait decades for justice is intellectual dishonesty that costs Nigerian lives.

Finally, the assertion that capital punishment violates “human rights” inverts morality and betrays victims. The foremost human right is the right to life. When the state refuses to execute those who deliberately and repeatedly violate that right on a massive scale, it sides with the destroyer against the destroyed. International NGOs and European governments that enjoy safe streets yet pressure Nigeria to abandon its Constitution and public will reveal a hypocrisy Nigerian victims instantly recognize. Their suffering is not an abstract data point for Geneva seminars; it is daily reality. Against that reality, the abolitionist position stands exposed as morally obscene.

### A Program of Active Enforcement
Nigeria should move immediately to active enforcement of the death penalty for terrorism resulting in death, aggravated murder, armed robbery resulting in death, kidnapping with lethal outcome, and treasonable offenses involving mass casualties. This requires:

- Swift trials in specialized courts with modern evidence rules.
- Mandatory death sentences upon conviction for qualifying offenses, with automatic but time-bound appeals.
- Public execution by firing squad or hanging for maximum deterrent visibility, conducted with dignity and recorded to prevent abuse.
- Transparent reporting of cases, evidence, and appeals to build public confidence.
- Parallel investment in police forensics, judicial capacity, and prison reform so that the system can reliably separate the guilty from the innocent.

The Nigerian people have suffered enough. The blood of schoolchildren slaughtered in their classrooms, farmers butchered in their fields, worshippers massacred at Pentecost, and families wiped out in Plateau demands a response proportionate to the evil inflicted. Sentimentality dressed up as sophistication has no place here. The state exists to protect the innocent and punish the guilty. When the guilty have committed crimes of ultimate wickedness, the only proportionate punishment is death.

Nigeria should not merely retain the death penalty on its statutes. It should apply it actively, justly, and without apology. The survival of the nation, the security of its people, fidelity to democratic legitimacy, obedience to its own Constitution, and justice for its countless victims require nothing less.

transcript.subject_reply:
**The Imperative of Capital Punishment in Nigeria: A Firm Defense of the Death Penalty for Serious Crimes**

Nigeria faces a moral emergency. Armed robbery, Boko Haram terrorism, banditry, kidnapping for ransom, and aggravated murder have turned vast swathes of the country into killing fields. Thousands are slaughtered every year. Communities are erased. The economy hemorrhages. Citizens live in constant, justified terror. In this blood-soaked reality, the sentimental abolition of capital punishment is not compassion—it is complicity. Active, consistent, and uncompromising enforcement of the death penalty for terrorism, armed robbery resulting in death, aggravated murder, lethal kidnapping, and mass atrocities is a moral, practical, and national imperative. Anything less betrays the living and desecrates the dead.

### Retribution and Moral Clarity
Certain crimes represent absolute evil. The deliberate massacre of civilians, the beheading of innocents, the systematic rape and murder of children, and the orchestrated destruction of entire villages annihilate the very idea of shared humanity. For these offenses, only the ultimate punishment fits. Execution is not revenge; it is the state’s clear, proportionate declaration that some acts are so monstrous their perpetrators forfeit the right to breathe the same air as their victims. Keeping such monsters alive in Nigerian prisons—at public expense, often while they continue directing operations—is an obscene insult to every life they have destroyed. Nigeria’s own traditional and customary systems long understood this truth. Imported Western abolitionism, preached from safe capitals by those whose streets are not running with blood, has no moral authority here. Retributive justice is not optional. It is the first demand of any decent society.

### Victim Justice and Closure for Survivors
The case for capital punishment is written in the blood of Nigeria’s victims. The 276 Chibok schoolgirls abducted in 2014, many still missing or enslaved years later. The Dapchi girls taken in 2018. The Pentecost Sunday massacre in Owo in 2022, where terrorists slaughtered more than forty worshippers inside their church. The Plateau Christmas massacres of 2023 that left around two hundred dead in coordinated attacks on farming communities. The endless bandit raids across the Northwest that have wiped out entire families—parents executed, children burned alive, survivors left to bury the dead while killers negotiate amnesty or escape through broken prisons.

These families do not want platitudes about rehabilitation or restorative justice. They demand finality. They deserve to know that the men who orchestrated the rape of their daughters, the butchering of their congregations, and the extermination of their villages have been lawfully removed from existence. Life imprisonment in Nigeria is a sick joke—prison breaks, political deals, and smuggled phones allow monsters to continue their work. Abolitionists who insist these perpetrators must be preserved at taxpayer expense while victims receive nothing but hollow condolences are not defending human dignity. They are practicing a grotesque moral inversion that places the comfort of mass killers above the anguish of their victims. Victim justice must come first. In Nigeria today, victim justice demands the rope, the firing squad, and the grave.

### Democratic Legitimacy and the Will of the Nigerian People
This demand is not imposed by a vocal minority. It is the settled will of the Nigerian people. Multiple NOIPolls and other independent surveys have consistently shown that more than 70 percent—and often over 80 percent—of Nigerians support the death penalty for terrorism, armed robbery, kidnapping, and aggravated murder. This majority cuts across ethnicity, religion, and region. From Lagos markets to displaced persons camps in the Northeast, ordinary citizens demand that the state execute those who slaughter their families.

In a democracy, that should end the debate. Public opinion on matters of life, death, and public safety is not an inconvenience to be managed by elites. It is the source of legitimacy. Abolition is not a homegrown Nigerian reform. It is an anti-democratic imposition pushed by foreign governments, European-funded NGOs, and a thin layer of Western-educated activists who value approval from Geneva and Brussels more than the security and values of their own countrymen. These voices demand that Nigerian legislators and judges override what Nigerians themselves overwhelmingly want. Such arrogance must be rejected. Nigeria is a sovereign democracy. Its justice system must reflect the clear, repeated verdict of its own citizens. Active enforcement of capital punishment is not merely popular—it is democratically legitimate.

### Constitutional and Legal Foundations
This popular will is not constitutionally suspect. It is explicitly authorized by Nigeria’s supreme law. Section 33(1) of the 1999 Constitution declares: “Every person has a right to life, and no one shall be deprived intentionally of his life, save in execution of the sentence of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty in Nigeria.” The framers wrote this exception deliberately. The Supreme Court of Nigeria confirmed it in *Kalu v. State* (1998) 13 NWLR (Pt. 583) 1, upholding the death penalty and rejecting claims that it constitutes cruel or inhuman treatment.

The twelve northern states’ Sharia penal codes further embed capital punishment for serious crimes under Islamic law, reflecting the convictions of tens of millions of Nigerian Muslims. Abolitionists are not asking for policy tweaks. They demand wholesale constitutional amendment under the onerous requirements of Section 9, plus the overriding of religious laws freely adopted by sovereign states. That is not reform. It is constitutional subversion and cultural imperialism. The death penalty is not an embarrassing relic. It is woven into Nigeria’s foundational legal order. Nigeria must stop apologizing for its own Constitution.

### Deterrence and Incapacitation
Beyond justice and legitimacy lies cold necessity. Nigeria is not a wealthy, high-capacity state with perfect policing and instant trials. It is a nation where conviction rates are abysmal, prisons leak like sieves, and terrorists operate with near-impunity. In this environment, the credible threat of execution is one of the few tools capable of shifting the calculus of rational predators who weigh risk against reward.

History supports this. Periods of active executions in earlier decades correlated with lower urban robbery rates. Today’s de facto moratorium has coincided with an explosion of banditry, kidnapping, and mass terrorism. Bandit kings, Boko Haram emirs, and kidnap lords act with the reasonable belief that even if captured they will likely escape, be amnestied, or die peacefully in custody. Execution removes that illusion permanently.

More importantly, it delivers absolute incapacitation. A dead terrorist cannot recruit, cannot plan the next church slaughter, cannot break out and kill again. Nigeria’s revolving-door prisons and failed “de-radicalization” schemes have already returned too many killers to the battlefield. Execution ends the cycle with finality. Deterrence and incapacitation are not abstract theories here. They are matters of national survival.

### Demolition of Objections
Abolitionists call the death penalty barbaric. The real barbarism is forcing the mothers of the Chibok girls, the survivors of Owo, and the widows of Plateau to watch their tormentors breathe free air or live comfortably in prison while the graves of their loved ones remain fresh. Nigerian victims know barbarism. It has names, faces, and AK-47s. The state that recoils from executing the guilty but tolerates their continued existence betrays the innocent and mocks justice.

They raise the risk of error. Yet the perpetrators of filmed Boko Haram beheadings, documented Owo church attacks, and eyewitness-identified Plateau massacres leave no reasonable doubt. Hiding behind hypothetical miscarriages of justice to shield the clearly guilty is not prudence—it is moral paralysis that sacrifices real victims on the altar of perfection. Nigeria must improve its institutions, but it must never allow the perfect to become the enemy of the good when monsters walk among us.

The claim that poverty causes crime and therefore excuses punishment is a contemptible evasion. Every nation has poverty. Few endure Nigeria’s scale of industrialized slaughter. Socio-economic reform is necessary, but security is the prerequisite. Pretending otherwise while victims wait decades for justice is intellectual cowardice that costs more Nigerian lives.

The final abolitionist slogan—that capital punishment violates human rights—stands exposed as obscene. The primary human right is the right to life. When the state protects the lives of mass murderers while abandoning their victims, it perverts the very concept of rights. Foreign NGOs and European governments that enjoy peace yet pressure Nigeria to abandon its Constitution, its democracy, and its dead reveal staggering hypocrisy. Their theories dissolve the moment they are weighed against the corpses of Nigerian schoolchildren and worshippers. Against the suffering of real victims, the abolitionist position is not merely wrong. It is morally obscene.

### A Call to Action
The time for debate is over. Nigeria must move immediately to the active, visible, and systematic enforcement of capital punishment for all qualifying offenses. Specialized courts with modern evidence rules. Mandatory death sentences upon conviction. Automatic but strictly time-bound appeals. Public executions by firing squad or hanging, carried out with dignity and recorded to guarantee transparency. Full forensic and judicial investment running in parallel.

Every single day without this policy is a day victims are betrayed and future victims are created. The blood of Chibok, Dapchi, Owo, Plateau, and a thousand nameless villages demands an answer. Nigeria must protect the innocent, punish the guilty, and restore the moral order. It must apply the death penalty actively, justly, and without apology.

The survival of the nation, the security of its people, the demands of justice, the voice of its democracy, the text of its Constitution, and the memory of its dead permit no other course. Nigeria must choose justice over sentiment, victims over killers, and survival over surrender. The death penalty must not only remain on the statutes. It must be used—swiftly, certainly, and without hesitation—starting now.

transcript.subject_reply:
**Capital Punishment: A Just, Effective, and Necessary Response to Nigeria’s Security Crisis**

Nigeria is bleeding. Armed robbery, kidnapping for ransom, Boko Haram and bandit terrorism, and cold-blooded murders have turned daily life into a lottery of terror for millions of citizens. Farmers abandon their fields, students fear school, travelers avoid roads after dark, and parents live in perpetual anxiety that their children will be the next victims. In the face of this collapse of order, half-measures and sentimental objections to the death penalty are luxuries Nigeria can no longer afford. Capital punishment for armed robbery, kidnapping, terrorism, and aggravated murder is not merely defensible; it is a moral imperative, a practical necessity, and the only language ruthless criminals currently understand.

First, justice demands it. Retributive justice is not vengeance; it is the principled assertion that some crimes are so heinous they forfeit the perpetrator’s right to continued existence in a civilized society. When a gang of armed robbers invades a home, rapes the women, shoots the father in front of his children, and leaves the family destitute, they have not merely broken a law; they have severed the social contract. When Boko Haram fighters burn villages, slaughter worshippers in their mosques and churches, and abduct hundreds of schoolgirls into sexual slavery, they declare war on the Nigerian people. A society that responds to such atrocities with life imprisonment funded by the taxes of the victims’ relatives is not merciful; it is complicit in moral inversion. The death penalty restores proportionality. It tells every citizen that the state sees the full weight of innocent blood and will answer it with equal gravity. Nigerians who have buried loved ones or paid ransoms that bankrupt families understand this in their bones. Only distant elites and foreign NGOs can afford the luxury of pretending that the murderer’s life is worth more than the cumulative suffering he has inflicted.

Second, deterrence works when it is swift, certain, and severe; Nigeria currently offers none of those. The country’s broken criminal justice system, overcrowded prisons, and frequent escapes have turned even life sentences into revolving doors. Criminals calculate risk. When the expected cost of kidnapping a governor’s daughter or planting IEDs in a market is a few years in a crumbling prison from which one can bribe one’s way out, the calculus favors crime. Public executions, carried out after rigorous but fair trials, change that calculus dramatically. Historical and cross-national evidence from societies that have faced comparable waves of violent crime (Singapore’s strict drug and firearms regime, the sharp drops in certain violent offenses in parts of the United States during periods of consistent capital sentencing, and even Nigeria’s own experience in the 1970s–80s when armed robbers were publicly executed) shows that potential offenders do respond to the prospect of death. In a country where poverty and unemployment are real but cannot excuse the deliberate choice to terrorize fellow citizens, the state must make the marginal cost of choosing the gun or the bomb unbearably high. Opponents who claim “there is no deterrent effect” are usually citing selective studies from stable Western democracies with functioning policing. They ignore the Nigerian reality: a weak state facing sophisticated, heavily armed criminal networks that treat leniency as weakness.

Third, Nigeria’s unique context makes abolitionist arguments dangerously naïve. The country faces not ordinary street crime but organized insurgency, banditry that controls territory, and commercial kidnapping that has become a parallel economy. These are not impulses of momentary passion; they are rational businesses. Boko Haram and bandit kingpins do not fear prison; many run their operations from inside poorly managed facilities. Only the finality of execution removes them permanently from circulation and disrupts command structures. Moreover, the Nigerian state owes a primary duty to the innocent victims and potential victims, not to the comfort of convicted terrorists. Every time a kidnapped child dies in captivity or a farmer is beheaded for refusing to pay “tax” to bandits, the state’s refusal to impose the ultimate penalty on previous offenders becomes an indirect contributor to the next atrocity. Mercy toward the merciless is cruelty toward the innocent.

Critics will raise the possibility of error. This is a serious concern that must be met with rigorous safeguards: independent forensic laboratories, mandatory appeals to the Court of Appeal and Supreme Court, strict rules against confessional statements obtained under duress, and competent legal representation funded by the state. These reforms are overdue regardless of one’s position on capital punishment. But the impossibility of perfect justice does not require the abandonment of all justice. We do not abolish surgery because patients sometimes die on the operating table; we improve standards and proceed because the alternative (letting cancer kill unchecked) is worse. Similarly, the rare risk of executing an innocent person, while tragic, does not outweigh the certain and repeated slaughter of innocents that occurs when the state signals it lacks the will to impose ultimate justice. In any event, the categories proposed—armed robbery with violence, kidnapping for ransom, terrorism, and aggravated murder involving rape, torture, or multiple victims—can be defined with sufficient precision to minimize error while covering the worst offenses.

The moralistic claim that “we should not descend to the level of the criminals” deserves particular contempt in the Nigerian context. The state does not “descend” when it executes a terrorist after due process; it affirms the supremacy of civilized law over barbarism. The kidnapper who buries a victim alive to extract payment from a grieving mother has already chosen the lowest level. The state that refuses to execute such a person does not occupy higher moral ground; it simply abandons its most vulnerable citizens. True morality weighs the life of the innocent child yet to be kidnapped against the life of the man who has already chosen to monetize human suffering. That weighing clearly favors the innocent.

Nigeria does not need more slogans, more international conferences on “restorative justice,” or more imported ideologies that have not passed the test of our reality. It needs a restored social order in which citizens can farm, trade, study, and travel without fear. That order requires credible threats against those who would destroy it. Capital punishment, applied transparently, justly, and without corruption after the highest level of judicial scrutiny, is an indispensable part of any serious strategy to reclaim Nigeria from the forces of chaos.

The time for squeamishness is over. The graves are multiplying. The hostages are still in the forests. The bandits are still collecting tribute. A responsible government must choose the living over the dead, the innocent over the guilty, and the future of thirty million schoolchildren over the continued breathing room of those who have made Nigeria a byword for insecurity. The death penalty is not the only tool needed; effective policing, judicial reform, economic opportunity, and intelligence-driven counter-terrorism are all essential. But among the tools of justice, capital punishment for the worst crimes is both the oldest and, in Nigeria’s present emergency, one of the most necessary. The state exists first to protect the innocent. In Nigeria today, that protection requires the gallows.
